config_setup.strategy: Task is a tiny, well-scoped surgical edit to two near-identical files, but with real correctness traps: CRLF preservation, legacy HTML, byte-level minimality, matching the site's existing `<time>` markup pattern, and EN-JP parity. The executor can do the edit directly; the highest-value single delegation (only one allowed) is an independent verifier that catches CRLF corruption, collateral row/text changes, and parity drift before finishing. So: one worker (reviewer). No author/coordinator split needed given the small edit surface; a second/third agent would only add tokens.

agents.description: Verifies the CRLF-safe <time> repair on en/jp home pages: minimal correct diff, no collateral changes, EN-JP parity. Use after edits are applied.
agents.name: edit-reviewer
agents.prompt:
You are a strict reviewer for a two-file surgical HTML edit. Do NOT modify files; report findings only.

Verify in BOTH en/index.html and jp/index.html:
1. The newest 2026.02.23 news row now has a native `<time datetime="2026-02-23">` wrapping its date text, matching the existing `<time>` markup pattern used by other rows (grep to compare attributes/spacing/casing).
2. Visible text, links, row order, and layout are unchanged; no other row was touched.
3. CRLF line endings are preserved (check for any lines that lost \r or any \n-only lines introduced). Legacy/quirky HTML is byte-identical except the single wrapped date.
4. The change is symmetric across en and jp (EN-JP parity).
5. No edits outside these two files.

Use `git diff` and targeted Grep/Read. Report PASS or a concise numbered list of exact problems with file:line. Do not restate what is correct beyond a one-line PASS summary.

config_setup.strategy: Task is tiny and precise: two known HTML files, one link each, exact attribute set (href, target, type, rel noopener noreferrer) and exact bilingual labels. The executor can do all edits itself with Read/Edit; parallel per-language editors add no value. The real failure modes are precision: dropping a rel token, wrong type, mismatched/mistyped labels (full-width （PDF）), line-ending/encoding drift, or touching layout. So spend the single allowed delegation on one verification worker that audits both files against the spec after edits. Sonnet at low effort is sufficient for a read-only diff check; no second/third agent justified.

agents.prompt:
Read en/member/yokota.html and jp/member/yokota.html. For the CV download link in EACH file, verify ALL of the following and report a PASS/FAIL checklist per file with the exact offending text on any FAIL:
- href="../../cv/cv.pdf"
- target="_blank"
- type="application/pdf"
- rel contains BOTH tokens: noopener AND noreferrer
- accessible label text: EN file exactly 'Download CV (PDF)'; JP file exactly '履歴書（PDF）をダウンロード' with full-width （PDF）, not ASCII (PDF)
- element location/layout unchanged vs surrounding markup (flag any restyling or moved element)
- no line-ending or encoding change: note if the file uses CRLF and whether it appears consistent
Do not edit any file. Report only findings; be terse.

config_setup.strategy: Single-file, spec-backed bug fix (js/analytics-consent.js). A capable executor with full tools could do this alone, so heavy delegation would only add tokens. The one genuine leverage point is the spec: it encodes exact cookie names, storage keys, event ordering, focus target, and translation keys that the fix must satisfy precisely. So I define ONE read-only worker that reads the (possibly long) spec + current file in an isolated context and returns a tight behavioral contract + minimal change plan, keeping the executor's context clean and reducing guess-and-check. The executor retains ownership of the only authorized edit and does the implementation + verification itself. A reviewer/second worker is not added because the executor delegates only once (a post-edit review couldn't be dispatched), and a third agent would add coordination cost with no capability gain on a lone-file task. Sonnet at medium effort is sufficient for focused spec distillation. EN-JP/line-ending precautions are included lightly because "preserve translations" implies i18n strings (likely EN/JP) and the edit must not reformat or re-encode.
